intro.heading: The origin
body: This framework didn't come from a single insight. It built gradually, out of a question I kept asking myself: why do businesses solving the same problem look so different from each other?

I wasn't just curious. I wanted a scientific way to map those differences. A configuration system that could explain, with precision, why one business delivers value in person while another delivers it through a platform, or why one charges a flat fee while another bills by the hour.

As I analyzed those businesses, patterns emerged. The same variables kept appearing, just set differently. I started calling them levers. And the more I looked, the more I realized they weren't random — they were a system.

One book that sharpened my thinking was Alex Hormozi's $100M Offers. Hormozi talks about delivery methods — do-it-yourself, done-with-you, done-for-you — and about divergent thinking: the idea that one input can produce many outputs depending on how you approach it. That combination clicked something into place for me. If you laid out all the levers, you could take a single business idea and generate variations of it systematically. That's where the mixer came in.

intro.heading: The mixer analogy
body: Think of a sound mixing console. You have inputs: instruments, vocals, whatever the band is playing. You have a console full of knobs, faders, and switches. And you have an output: the final mix, shaped by how every control is set.
Change one knob, and the output changes. Same inputs, different configuration, different results.

A business works the same way. The inputs are your audience, your core idea (what I call the Seed), and the outcomes you want to create for your customers. The levers are the controls: delivery model, pricing structure, how much you're involved, how the work gets done. The output is your business model: a specific variation of your idea, shaped by how every lever is set.

What makes this powerful is what I think of as the generator — the logic inside the console that, given any configuration, can produce variations of your idea. A DIY delivery model applied to "learning how to code" generates courses, guides, documentation, YouTube channels, books. Same lever setting, many different outputs. The generator is what turns a configuration into a real business.

You can use AI as that generator. But I'd encourage you to develop this understanding yourself first — to learn how each lever works, and what changes when you move it. That's what this chapter is about.

intro.heading: Before we dive in
body: A few things to keep in mind before we get into the levers.

First: this framework is a work in progress. It's shaped by my way of thinking about businesses, and by the businesses I've actually researched. I haven't studied every business in the world — no one has. So treat this as a useful map, not a complete one.

Second, and this one matters more: the levers themselves are not the point. The point is that there is a scientific method for building and iterating on a business. One that lets you take an initial idea, your Seed, and explore the full space of what it could become. That's what the mixer gives you.

Use it in two ways. First, to generate options: if you're not sure where to start, the levers help you map the different configurations your idea could take, and choose the one that makes the most sense right now. Second, to plan your evolution: a business rarely stays in the same configuration. Understanding the levers helps you see where you are today, and chart a deliberate path toward where you want to be.

For example, many founders start with a done-for-you model: you do the work, you charge a premium, you collect success stories. Over time, as your authority grows, you transition toward done-with-you or do-it-yourself. A DIY product requires trust. People need to believe that following your system will actually get them somewhere. That trust is built through track record, not declared upfront.

Finally: the values listed for each lever are not exhaustive. They're starting points, the most common configurations I've observed. You can find values in between, create hybrid combinations, or invent something new. The goal isn't to pick from a menu. It's to understand what each lever controls, so you can make intentional choices about how your business is built.

intro.heading: Two control types
body: Each lever is set with one of two controls.
Checkbox — the values stack. An offer can hold more than one at once, so check every one that genuinely applies, then decide which you lead with.
Slider — the values are points on a single scale. Pick the one position that fits; they are mutually exclusive.

intro.heading: What changed in this version
body: Delivery model, Source of value, Participation format, Delivery medium, and Pricing model moved from slider to checkbox — they are additive, not either/or.
Interaction timing and Price framing dropped their standalone "Hybrid" option — checking both poles is the hybrid.

levers.name: Delivery model
levers.question: Who is responsible for producing the outcome?
intro: The delivery model lever answers one question: who is responsible for producing the outcome?
If the outcome is "build a website," the delivery model determines whether you build it, whether you build it together with your client, or whether your client builds it using what you give them.

When you move this lever, you're changing: who does the work, who learns the most, your level of engagement with the outcome, how much you control whether the client actually gets there, and how you price the service.
There are many variations of this lever, including hybrids and custom combinations. But the three core values are: Done For You (DFY), Done With You (DWY), and Do It Yourself (DIY).

values.name: Done for you (DFY)
summary: You are responsible for delivering the outcome. The client pays, and the outcome is delivered to them.
It's the easiest model for your client and the hardest for you. But it's also where you have the most control over whether they actually achieve the result.

DFY is usually the most expensive model. It's also the best place to start when you're building authority. To sell a DIY product, people need to trust that your system works. That trust comes from a track record, and a track record comes from doing the work. Once you've done it enough times, you can say: "I've done this so many times that I built a tool so you can do it yourself." That's the natural progression.
The authority dynamic in DFY is clear: you're a provider working for the client, accountable for the result.

facets.whatChanges: Moving toward DFY increases delivery operations and your personal involvement. As you take on more responsibility, delivery becomes harder to delegate or automate. Client expectations shift accordingly: in DFY, they expect proof you can deliver, a track record, a guarantee, or both.
facets.whenConvenient: Early stage, when you're building authority and need proof-of-concept with real clients. Also the right model when the outcome is complex enough that clients can't reliably get there without you.
facets.examples: Agency work, productized services. "I build your website."
facets.traps: Staying in DFY too long. Many freelancers default to this model and then conclude that scaling is impossible. It isn't. DFY is a starting point, not a ceiling.
facets.worksWellWith: Authority dynamic — provider. Source of value — human. Interaction timing — async. Touch intensity — high. Buyer participation — passive. Higher price point.

values.name: Done with you (DWY)
summary: You guide, coach, or work alongside the client while they produce the outcome. You're not doing it for them, and you're not just teaching and stepping back. You work together: they do the work, you guide, correct, and accelerate.
"We build it together."

Using the "build your website" example: instead of building it for them, you sit with them — live or async — while they build. You help them choose the right tools. They create the pages, but you review and catch mistakes early. You give feedback on copy, structure, and design as they go. When they get stuck, you unblock them.
End result: the website gets built, they understand how it works, and they can update or rebuild it later without depending on you.

facets.whatChanges: DWY requires more from the client than DFY, but less provider time than full DFY. It's the middle ground for operations, energy, and price. Client expectations in DWY are about guidance: they expect you to have helped others through this before.
facets.whenConvenient: When your clients are genuinely willing to do the work and want to build the skill alongside the outcome. When you want to charge more than a DIY product but spend less time than DFY.
facets.examples: Coaching, advisory, group coaching, mastermind, workshop + live Q&A.
facets.traps: Designing a DWY offer for clients who secretly want DFY. If they're not actually willing to do the work, the model breaks, and you end up doing DFY work at DWY prices.
facets.worksWellWith: Participation format — 1:1, cohort, or group. Interaction timing — live or async. Touch intensity — medium-high.

values.name: Do it yourself (DIY)
summary: The client does everything on their own. You give them the knowledge, tools, and structure, then step back.
"You go build it, I'll show you how."

Using a "build your website" business: you give them everything upfront and they implement on their own. A step-by-step course. Templates. A checklist. Recorded tutorials. Little to no direct interaction with you during the process.
End result: the website may or may not get built. That depends entirely on them. They learn the skill if they follow through. No dependency on you.

facets.whatChanges: DIY is where scalability lives. The more you move toward DIY, the more you can scale, automate, and sell to many people at once. The trade-off: it's the lowest-priced model, and it has the lowest completion rate. Client expectations shift as well. In DIY, they're not buying your time, they're buying your framework. They expect it to be digestible, proven, and low-effort to follow.
facets.whenConvenient: When you have a track record to stand behind. When your audience is self-motivated and disciplined. When you want to scale without adding proportional time.
facets.examples: Async courses, templates, guides, content libraries. "Here's everything you need to build your website."
facets.traps:
Starting with DIY before you've built authority. If there's no track record, there's no reason to trust that following your system will actually get them somewhere. Build the track record first, then productize it.

One honest thing worth acknowledging: many of your clients will never finish. That's part of the model. Make peace with it before you build it.
facets.worksWellWith: Participation format — 1:n (async course, content). Interaction timing — async. Touch intensity — low. Source of value — system. Buyer participation — active (they have to do the work).

notes.heading: Hybrids and combinations
body: DIY, DWY, and DFY are starting points, not a complete menu. There are values in between, and hybrid combinations are not only valid, they're often where the most interesting models live.

A simple hybrid: DWY + DIY. "I show you how to do it, then you do it yourself." A webinar where you walk through a tool live, then hand it to the participants to implement. You were with them for the learning, but they carry the execution.
Where it gets genuinely interesting is when you combine delivery model with participation format. That combination alone produces most of the business models you've seen or built:

- DIY + 1:1 → self-serve + optional private support
- DIY + 1:cohort → cohort-based course
- DIY + 1:n → async course / content
- DWY + 1:1 → coaching / advisory
- DWY + 1:cohort → group coaching / mastermind
- DWY + 1:group → workshop + live Q&A
- DFY + 1:1 → service / agency
- DFY + 1:cohort → small batch service
- DFY + 1:n → productized service / scaled DFY system
This is where you let your divergent brain wander outer space. Just make sure it comes back!

levers.name: Source of value
levers.question: What is the main thing the client is really paying for?
intro: The source of value lever answers one question: what is the main thing the client is really paying for?
Not what the offer delivers — that's the delivery model. This is about what makes your offer worth buying in the first place. The actual driver behind the perceived value.
When you move this lever, you're changing: how you position the offer, what you lead with in your marketing, what you need to protect and strengthen, and how you compete.
Three core values: human, system, and asset.

values.name: Human
summary: The value comes mainly from you or your team. The client is paying for your judgment, creativity, expertise, taste, and ability to make good decisions.

Take a "build a website" business. Human-led: you talk with the client, understand their business, decide the right structure, copy direction, UX, and branding. You build something thoughtful and tailored. The client isn't mainly buying a website. They're buying your brain and hands.
"I'll design the right website for your business." "I'll help you clarify your messaging and structure before we build anything." "You'll get a custom site based on strategy, not just a template."

facets.whatChanges:
Human value is hard to scale and hard to fake. It commands higher prices because it's personal and scarce. But it's also the most fragile: if you're not available, the value disappears.

It's usually the right place to start. Direct client work gives you real feedback, sharpens your judgment, and makes your human value more valuable over time.
facets.whenConvenient: Early stage, when your track record is what earns the sale. When the problem is complex and context-dependent. When clients are paying for a result, not just a deliverable.
facets.examples: Custom web design, strategic consulting, bespoke creative work, personalized coaching.
facets.traps: Competing on personality rather than expertise. "You'll love working with me" isn't a value proposition. The value should be in what you know and what you produce, not just who you are.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DFY or DWY. Authority dynamic — expert or trusted advisor. Touch intensity — high.

values.name: System
summary: The value comes mainly from a process, tool, template, automation, or repeatable method. The client is paying for efficiency, consistency, speed, and reliability.

Same business, system-led: you use a proven framework for all websites. Pre-built sections, templates, SOPs, automations. The site gets built faster, cheaper, and more consistently because the system does the heavy lifting.
"Get your website live in 5 days with our proven process." "We use a repeatable website system for service businesses." "Choose your style, send your content, and we assemble the site fast."
The client isn't buying deep custom thinking. They're buying a solid result through an efficient machine.

facets.whatChanges:
System value scales. The more clients you serve, the better the system gets. Margins improve as delivery becomes faster and more predictable.

The trade-off: it's harder to command premium prices. If the value is the system, not you, clients can eventually find another system. Your competitive advantage is the quality, reliability, and reputation of the one you've built.
facets.whenConvenient: When you've run the process enough times to know it works. When speed and consistency matter more to your client than customization. When you want to serve more clients without proportionally growing your time.
facets.examples: Productized services, template-based design, website packages, SOP-driven agencies.
facets.traps: Selling a system before it's actually reliable. A system that breaks frequently is worse than no system — it creates expectations you then fail to meet. Build it quietly. Sell it publicly once it's proven.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DFY at scale or DIY. Pricing model — flat fee or package. Touch intensity — low to medium.

values.name: Asset
summary: The value comes mainly from something you already own or control. The client is paying for access to something valuable that already exists — not your time, and not a process.

Same business, asset-led: you build the site using your premium theme library, your photo library, your copy library, your component system. Or you place it inside your trusted brand ecosystem. Or you promote the site through your audience and connect it to your network after launch.
"We'll build your site on top of our premium design library." "You get access to our private conversion-tested blocks." "We feature your business in our partner network after launch."
The labor and the process are still there. But the main reason to buy from you specifically is the asset.

facets.whatChanges:
Asset value is the hardest to replicate. If you own something genuinely valuable — a distribution channel, a trusted brand, a proprietary library, a network — that becomes your real competitive advantage. Clients don't just buy the outcome. They buy access to leverage.

The challenge: assets take time to build. You can't manufacture them quickly. But once they exist, they compound. Every client you serve through the asset makes it more credible and more valuable.
facets.whenConvenient: When you've built something others don't have and can't easily copy. When the asset creates a visible, concrete advantage the client understands immediately. When "built by us" means something beyond the deliverable itself.
facets.examples: Agencies with proprietary tools or templates, publishers with built-in distribution, platforms with an existing audience, businesses with exclusive partnerships or networks.
facets.traps: Mistaking a tool for an asset. A Notion template isn't an asset unless it's genuinely unique and proven. An asset has to be valuable enough that a client would consider paying for access to it alone, independent of the rest of the offer.
facets.worksWellWith: Pricing model — premium or access-based. Authority dynamic — brand or platform. Delivery model — any.

notes.heading: How to tell the difference
body: Ask one question: if the client asks "Why should I buy this from you?", what's the strongest honest answer?
If the answer is "Because I'm good at this" → Human. If the answer is "Because the process works" → System. If the answer is "Because you get access to something valuable I already own" → Asset.
Most offers have more than one source of value, but usually one is primary. Three people selling "website building":

- Offer A: "I personally work with you to craft the right site for your business." Primary source of value: Human.
- Offer B: "We build your site in one week using our proven system." Primary source of value: System.
- Offer C: "We build your site using our premium brand kit, private templates, and partner distribution network." Primary source of value: Asset.
Same category. Different value source. Different positioning, pricing, and competitive moat.

whyItMatters: It forces you to answer a strategic question: what is the real thing making this offer worth paying for?

Once you know that, everything else gets clearer. You market the right thing. You price based on the right driver. You invest in strengthening the right part of the offer. And you stop mixing promises that confuse the client about what they're actually buying.

levers.name: Interaction timing
levers.question: When does value happen?
intro: The interaction timing lever answers one question: when does value happen?

Not what you deliver, and not who does the work — this lever is about the rhythm of the relationship. Does the client interact with you and your offer in real time, on their own schedule, or a mix of both?
When you move this lever, you're changing: client experience, scheduling complexity, speed of delivery, how easy the offer is to scale, and how much momentum the engagement generates.
Three core values: synchronous, asynchronous, and hybrid.

values.name: Synchronous
summary: The client and provider interact in real time. Both parties need to be present at the same time for value to happen.

For a "build a website" business: "We jump on a 90-minute live call and plan your website together." You're mapping the structure in real time, making decisions on the spot, and walking away with clarity that would have taken weeks of back-and-forth to reach.
The value comes from speed, momentum, and the quality of decisions that happen when two people are thinking through a problem together.

There's a less obvious benefit too: you see the client going through the process. If you're delivering a weight loss program and you're with your client as they exercise and navigate their diet, you observe where they struggle, where they cheat, where they get confused. That's information you can't get from a form submission. Synchronous delivery makes you better at what you do, faster.

facets.whatChanges:
Synchronous delivery is harder to scale — it requires scheduling, and both parties' time has to align. But it generates something asynchronous rarely does: energy. A live session creates momentum that carries the project forward. Clients who feel stuck often just need a live conversation.

It also commands higher perceived value. Real-time access to an expert feels different from receiving a recorded Loom.
facets.whenConvenient: When the work involves complex decisions that need immediate input. When the client relationship benefits from real human presence. When momentum is part of what you're selling. High-touch offers, custom work, coaching.
facets.examples: Strategy calls, live design sessions, workshops, onboarding calls, real-time feedback sessions.
facets.traps: Defaulting to synchronous because it feels more professional. Live time is expensive — yours and your client's. Use it where it genuinely creates more value than async would. If you're spending an hour live doing something a Loom could handle in ten minutes, that's a design problem.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DWY. Touch intensity — high. Source of value — human. Participation format — 1:1 or small group. Scope breadth — wide (more ground to cover together).

values.name: Asynchronous
summary: Interaction happens on each party's own schedule. No real-time presence required — the client sends their content, feedback, or questions when it's convenient for them, and you respond when it's convenient for you.

Same business: "Send your content through our intake form and receive updates by Loom." The client fills in a brief on Sunday night. You record a walkthrough on Monday morning. They leave feedback by Tuesday. The website gets built without a single meeting.
The value comes from flexibility, convenience, and documentation. Everything is written down or recorded, which means fewer misunderstandings and a cleaner trail.

facets.whatChanges:
Async delivery is scalable in the clearest sense of the word: while one client is reviewing your Loom, another is filling your intake form, and a third is implementing your feedback. You're not context-switching between live sessions — your time compounds. You can serve more clients simultaneously without adding hours to your day.

It's also friendlier to busy clients and different time zones.

The trade-off is pace and energy. Decisions take longer when they happen in rounds. Misalignments that a three-minute live conversation would resolve can linger for days. And some clients — especially those who aren't sure what they want — need real-time dialogue to clarify their thinking. Async doesn't give them that.
facets.whenConvenient: When your clients are self-directed and can articulate what they need in writing. When speed of decision isn't critical. When you want to serve more clients without expanding your calendar. Repeatable delivery, global audiences, scalable services.
facets.examples: Email-based feedback, Loom walkthroughs, shared documents, form-based intake, task boards, content delivered anytime.
facets.traps: Using async as a cost-cutting workaround rather than a deliberate design choice. If your clients need live conversation to feel confident in the process, removing it doesn't make you efficient — it makes you frustrating. Match the timing to what your client actually needs to move forward.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DIY or scaled DFY. Source of value — system. Touch intensity — low to medium. Participation format — 1:n.

notes.heading: How to identify it
body: Ask: when does the client need to show up?
Mostly live meetings → Synchronous. Mostly messages and files at their own pace → Asynchronous. Some of both, intentionally → Hybrid.
The same offer can use all three timing configurations and produce very different client experiences. Three people building websites:
Offer A: "We co-build your site in a one-day live session." → Synchronous.
Offer B: "Fill our intake form, receive your finished site in 7 days." → Asynchronous.
Offer C: "Kickoff call to align, async build, final review call together." → Hybrid.
Same category. Same deliverable. Completely different rhythms.

whyItMatters: Interaction timing shapes how clients experience your offer more than almost any other lever. It affects whether they feel taken care of or left alone, whether decisions happen fast or drag out, whether your calendar runs your business or your business runs your calendar.
Get it right and the offer feels effortless. Get it wrong and clients feel either overwhelmed by meetings or abandoned between them.

levers.name: Participation format
levers.question: How many people benefit from one unit of your effort?
intro: The participation format lever answers one question: how many people benefit from one unit of your effort?
Not how many clients you have in total — this is about the structure of delivery itself. When you sit down to do the work, how many people receive the value that comes out of it?

When you move this lever, you're changing: how much you can charge per client, how many clients you can serve, how much personalization is possible, how fast trust is built, and how heavy the support burden becomes.
Four core values: 1:1, 1:group, 1:community, and 1:n.

summary: You deliver value to one person at a time. One hour of your effort reaches one client.

For a "teach people to build their own website" business: a private session where you walk one founder through the entire process — their specific content, their goals, their platform, their design decisions. Fully personalized from start to finish.
This is the highest-intimacy, lowest-scale configuration. It's also where you build trust the fastest, because the client has your full attention.

facets.whatChanges:
1:1 commands the highest price per client. Personalization is the product, and personalization can't be replicated at scale without degrading. The trade-off is obvious: there are only so many hours in your week.

It's also the highest-feedback format. You see exactly where each client struggles, what questions they ask, what they skip, what confuses them. That information is worth more than most people realize when you're building your offer.
facets.whenConvenient: When you're still learning what your clients need. When the outcome requires custom adaptation. When the price point justifies the time investment. When trust needs to be built quickly.
facets.examples: Private coaching, 1:1 consulting, personalized advisory, done-with-you sessions.
facets.traps: Staying in 1:1 indefinitely because it feels good. The intimacy is real, but at some point it becomes a ceiling. Your income depends entirely on your availability, and that's a fragile position to be in.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DFY or DWY. Touch intensity — high. Interaction timing — synchronous or hybrid. Source of value — human.

values.name: 1:group
summary: You deliver value to a small, defined group at the same time. One session reaches multiple clients at once.

Same business: a live workshop where 15 founders learn to build their websites together. Same session, same material — but the clients are in the room together, asking questions that benefit everyone, seeing that others have the same doubts they do.
The group dynamic adds something 1:1 can't: shared experience. Clients learn from each other, not just from you.

facets.whatChanges:
1:group gives you leverage without sacrificing too much personalization. You're still present, still responsive — just not exclusively focused on one person. Revenue per hour goes up because the same session generates multiple client fees.

The coordination overhead increases. Scheduling a group is harder than scheduling a private session, and you're managing group dynamics, not just a single client relationship.
facets.whenConvenient: When the learning experience benefits from peers in the room. When you want more leverage than 1:1 but more intimacy than a recorded course. When cohort momentum is a feature, not just a side effect.
facets.examples: Live workshops, cohort courses, group coaching, masterminds, live Q&A sessions.
facets.traps:
Under-pricing because it feels cheaper than 1:1. Group delivery isn't less valuable — in many cases it's more valuable, because the peer dynamic accelerates results. Price accordingly.

When working with groups or cohorts, one of the main limits is that people should be dealing with the same problem at the same time. That is what makes the group valuable for everyone. If the group is too mixed, some people will be bored because they already know the topic, while others will feel lost because they are just starting.

Using our 'I help you build a website' example, some people may already know their offer, messaging, and direction, while others may still be figuring out the basics.

Another challenge is filling the cohort with paying customers. People usually join when the value proposition is clear and strong, and when the timing feels right for them. If your message is weak or they are not ready now, they may not buy.

Because of this, cohorts often need a steady flow of leads and good timing. If not, other models like 1:1 work, workshops, or rolling programs may work better.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DWY. Interaction timing — synchronous or hybrid. Touch intensity — medium to high. Source of value — human or system.

values.name: 1:community
summary: You deliver value into an ongoing ecosystem where many people participate, interact with each other, and engage when it's relevant to them.

Same business: a founder community built around website building. There are training modules. There are channels where members share their work and get feedback from peers. There are live sessions when you run them, but the value doesn't stop when the session ends — members keep helping each other, answering questions, sharing what's working.
The key difference from 1:group: the community keeps generating value without requiring your active presence in every moment.

facets.whatChanges:
Community compounds over time. The more members, the richer the peer knowledge base, the stronger the network, the more valuable membership becomes. Your effort as the host becomes more leveraged as the community grows — a great answer from a senior member benefits everyone who asks a similar question later.

The complexity of running it also grows. A community is a living thing. It needs structure, moderation, consistent energy, and a reason for members to keep showing up. Neglected communities collapse fast.
facets.whenConvenient: When peer learning is a meaningful part of the value. When your clients benefit from ongoing access, not just a one-time result. When you want a recurring revenue model with compounding network effects.
facets.examples: Paid membership communities, alumni networks, ongoing programs with community layers, forum-based learning platforms.
facets.traps: Building a community when your clients actually want a course. Not everyone wants to participate in a group — some just want to learn the thing and move on. Make sure ongoing engagement is something your client actually values before you invest in the infrastructure.
facets.worksWellWith: Interaction timing — hybrid (some live, much async). Participation — active. Pricing model — subscription. Delivery model — DWY or DIY.

values.name: 1:n
summary: You deliver value publicly, to an unlimited or very large audience. One piece of effort — a course, a video, a template, a guide — can reach anyone, anytime, without you being involved.

Same business: a recorded course teaching website building. Or a YouTube channel with the full process. Or a template pack anyone can buy. You make it once. It reaches thousands. You're not there when they use it.
This is the highest-scale, lowest-personalization configuration. You're not selling your time or your presence. You're selling a system or an asset that travels without you.

facets.whatChanges:
1:n decouples your income from your calendar. Once the content exists, it can generate revenue while you sleep, take a week off, or work on the next thing. Margins improve dramatically over time as the upfront creation cost gets amortized across more and more buyers.

The trade-off: trust is slower to build. Clients can't ask you questions, can't adapt the material to their situation, and can't get real-time help when they're stuck. Completion rates are low. That's not a failure of the format — it's how it works. Make peace with it before you build it.
facets.whenConvenient: When you have a track record strong enough to sell without being in the room. When the problem is predictable enough that a static solution works for most people. When you want to reach audiences that would never pay for 1:1 or group access.
facets.examples: Recorded courses, YouTube channels, ebooks, template packs, public content libraries.
facets.traps: Starting here before you've done the 1:1 and 1:group versions. A 1:n product is a systematized version of knowledge you should already have from direct client work. Build the track record first. Productize it second.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DIY. Interaction timing — async. Touch intensity — low. Source of value — system or asset. Pricing model — one-time or subscription.

notes.heading: How to identify it
body: Ask: how many people benefit from one hour of your effort?
One person → 1:1. A small live group → 1:group. An ongoing member ecosystem → 1:community. An unlimited public audience → 1:n.
Same DIY offer, four configurations:
Offer A: teach one founder to build their website in a private session. → 1:1.
Offer B: run a live workshop for 20 founders building their sites together. → 1:group.
Offer C: build an ongoing community where founders learn, share, and support each other. → 1:community.
Offer D: publish a course anyone can buy and work through on their own. → 1:n.
Same knowledge. Very different reach, intimacy, and business model.

whyItMatters: Participation format is one of the most direct levers you have on scalability. Move it and you change how many people you can serve, how much you can charge, and what kind of relationship the client has with the offer.

It's also one of the easiest to evolve. Most founders start with 1:1 because that's where you learn the most. Then they layer in 1:group once the patterns are clear. Then 1:n once the process is proven. The formats aren't mutually exclusive — many strong offers stack them deliberately.

levers.name: Delivery medium
levers.question: What does the client actually interact with to receive the value?
intro: The delivery medium lever answers one question: what does the client actually interact with to receive the value?

Not why the offer is worth paying for — that's the source of value. Not who delivers it — that's the delivery model. This lever is specifically about the format or channel through which value travels from you to the client.
When you move this lever, you're changing: what the client experiences, what you have to build and maintain, what margins look like, and how the offer scales.
Three core values: presence-based, asset-based, and tool-based.

values.name: Presence-based
summary: The client receives value through direct interaction with a person. Live calls, workshops, consulting sessions, real-time collaboration. The medium is human presence itself.

For a "build a website" business: "We meet live and plan your website together." The client isn't watching a video or clicking through a tool — they're in the room with you, and value happens through that interaction.

facets.whatChanges:
Presence-based delivery is the most personal and the most time-constrained. You can't separate the value from the moment it's delivered — when the session ends, so does the medium. That limits how many clients you can serve, but it also creates the highest perceived intimacy and the strongest foundation for trust.

It also gives you the richest client feedback. You see reactions in real time. You adjust. That learning compounds into everything else you build.
facets.whenConvenient: When personal interaction is central to the outcome. When the client needs to be guided, not just informed. When the offer is positioned around access to you specifically.
facets.examples: Live calls, workshops, in-person sessions, consulting engagements, real-time co-working.
facets.traps: Treating presence as the only legitimate medium. Live delivery isn't more valuable by default — it's just more personal. Some outcomes are better served through assets or tools. Use presence where it earns its place.
facets.worksWellWith: Interaction timing — synchronous. Touch intensity — high. Source of value — human. Participation format — 1:1 or 1:group.

values.name: Asset-based
summary: The client receives value through reusable materials — recorded content, templates, written guides, checklists, SOPs, swipe files. The medium is something that exists independently of you and can be consumed at any time.
Same business: "Buy our website kit with templates and step-by-step lessons." You made it once. The client uses it when it fits their schedule, at their own pace, as many times as they need.

facets.whatChanges:
Asset-based delivery decouples value from your availability. Once the asset exists, it can serve one client or ten thousand. Margins improve significantly as the upfront creation cost gets spread across more buyers.

The trade-off is intimacy and adaptability. An asset can't read the room, adjust to a client's specific situation, or catch a subtle misunderstanding before it derails progress. It delivers what it delivers, consistently.
facets.whenConvenient: When the knowledge or method is stable enough to package well. When clients are self-directed enough to use materials without guidance. When you want to reach audiences at price points that presence-based delivery can't serve profitably.
facets.examples: Recorded courses, template packs, written guides, playbooks, content libraries, email sequences.
facets.traps: Building assets before testing the content live. The best assets are distillations of knowledge you've already delivered in presence-based contexts — you know which explanations land, which examples resonate, which steps people skip. Build the asset from experience, not assumptions.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DIY or scaled DFY. Interaction timing — async. Touch intensity — low to medium. Participation format — 1:n.

values.name: Tool-based
summary: The client receives value through software, a platform, or an interactive product. The medium is the tool itself — they use it, and the output is the result.
Same business: "Use our platform to create your website automatically." The client doesn't need your time or your materials. They interact with what you've built, and the tool produces the outcome.

facets.whatChanges:
Tool-based delivery is the highest-leverage medium at scale. One tool can serve millions of clients without proportional increases in cost or time. Margins compound as the user base grows.

The trade-off is investment. Building a reliable, well-designed tool requires capital, engineering, and ongoing maintenance. The bar for "good enough" is higher too — a bad live session is forgettable, but a buggy tool destroys trust fast.
facets.whenConvenient: When the outcome is repeatable and predictable enough that software can produce it reliably. When you have the resources to build and maintain the tool. When the audience is large enough to justify the upfront investment.
facets.examples: SaaS platforms, AI generators, website builders, interactive wizards, automated dashboards, apps.
facets.traps: Underestimating what it takes to build a tool that actually works. Many founders jump from "I have a repeatable process" to "I should build software" without realizing how much of their current delivery depends on human judgment that software can't replicate yet. Start with assets and presence. Move to tools when the process is genuinely mechanical.
facets.worksWellWith: Source of value — system or asset. Delivery model — DIY or scaled DFY. Touch intensity — low. Interaction timing — async. Participation format — 1:n.

notes.heading: Delivery medium vs. source of value
body: These two levers get confused because they often move together. They don't have to.
Source of value answers: why does the client want this? Delivery medium answers: what do they interact with to get it?

The same source of value can travel through different media. If your expertise is what the client is buying, you can deliver it through a live session (presence-based), a recorded course (asset-based), or software that embeds your framework (tool-based). Same source, different medium.

And the same medium can carry different sources of value. An asset-based course might be worth buying because of your expertise, your brand, the speed of implementation, or the exclusive templates inside. Same medium, different source.
Three people building websites makes this concrete:
Offer A: "I personally guide you live." Source of value — human. Delivery medium — presence-based.
Offer B: "My recorded program shows you exactly how." Source of value — human. Delivery medium — asset-based.
Offer C: "Use my AI builder trained on proven layouts." Source of value — system. Delivery medium — tool-based.
The simple version: source of value is why they buy. Delivery medium is how they receive it.

whyItMatters: The same value can be packaged in completely different ways, and that packaging changes everything downstream: margins, scalability, price point, client experience, and what you have to build to deliver it. Choosing the right medium isn't just an operational decision — it's a strategic one.

levers.name: Pricing model
levers.question: What logic determines how the buyer pays?
intro: The pricing model lever answers one question: what logic determines how the buyer pays?
Not how much they pay — that's a number. This lever is about the mechanism. The formula. When the invoice is generated, what determines what's on it?

When you move this lever, you're changing: how easy the offer is to buy, how predictable your revenue is, who absorbs the risk of scope changes, how aligned your incentives are with the client's outcomes, and how your business scales.
Four core values: fixed, variable, time-based, and recurring.

values.name: Fixed
summary: A predetermined price for a defined offer. The buyer knows the number before they commit.
For a "build a website" business: "5-page website for $2,500." The scope is set. The price is set. No surprises on either side.

Fixed pricing is the simplest to sell and the simplest to buy. The decision is binary — the buyer either agrees to the price or they don't. There's no negotiation over hours, no ambiguity about what's included, no invoice that lands differently than expected.

facets.whatChanges:
Fixed pricing forces clarity. You have to know your scope well enough to name a price with confidence. That's a useful discipline — if you can't define the offer clearly enough to price it, you don't have an offer yet, you have a conversation.

The risk sits with you. If the project takes longer than expected, you absorb the cost. That's appropriate when your process is well-defined enough to estimate reliably. When it isn't, fixed pricing becomes a liability — you either underprice or pad the quote to compensate for uncertainty.
facets.whenConvenient: When the offer has a clear, repeatable scope you've delivered enough times to price with confidence. When you want fast sales cycles and minimal negotiation. When the buyer values predictability and you can deliver it.
facets.examples: Productized services, set-price packages, flat-fee audits, fixed-scope builds.
facets.traps: Pricing fixed work based on optimistic estimates. Every project that comes in under budget is a gift. Every project that runs over is a problem you're funding. Price fixed offers based on realistic averages, not best-case scenarios.
facets.worksWellWith: Scope breadth — narrow to medium. Scope depth — defined. Effort duration — one-time or time-bound. Commitment reversibility — medium.

values.name: Variable
summary: Price changes based on scope, complexity, usage, results, or some combination. The final number depends on inputs that aren't fully determined at the time of sale.

Same business: "Website starts at $1,500. Final price depends on number of pages and integrations needed." The buyer knows the floor. They understand the logic. But the invoice is shaped by what the project actually requires.

Variable pricing captures complexity. It's the honest answer when the work genuinely varies — when some clients need three pages and some need fifteen, and charging them the same would mean one subsidizes the other.

facets.whatChanges:
Variable pricing requires a clear conversation upfront about what drives the price and how it's calculated. Buyers can handle variability when they understand the logic. What they can't handle is surprise — an invoice that lands significantly higher than expected, for reasons they didn't see coming.

It also requires disciplined scope tracking during delivery. If the price changes as the project evolves, both sides need to be tracking those changes in real time, not reconciling them at the end.
facets.whenConvenient: When the offer genuinely varies in complexity across clients. When charging a flat rate would consistently over- or under-price a meaningful portion of your work. When your ideal clients are sophisticated enough to understand and accept variable pricing logic.
facets.examples: Custom development, consulting engagements with evolving scope, usage-based SaaS pricing, performance-based fees, custom quote services.
facets.traps: Using variable pricing to avoid the hard work of defining your offer. "It depends" is sometimes honest, but it's also sometimes a symptom of not knowing your own costs or not having a repeatable process. Every time you quote a custom project from scratch, you're spending time that fixed pricing eliminates. Move toward fixed where you can.
facets.worksWellWith: Scope breadth — medium to broad. Scope depth — variable. Source of value — human. Delivery model — DFY with scope discovery.

values.name: Time-based
summary: The buyer pays for time — hours, days, or blocks of availability. The invoice reflects how long the work took.
Same business: "$120 per hour for website strategy and build support." The client pays for the time you spend, not the outcome you produce. More time, more cost. Less time, lower bill.

Time-based pricing is honest when scope is genuinely fluid. It's the right answer when the client's problem isn't well-defined enough to scope upfront, or when they need access to your judgment rather than a defined deliverable.

facets.whatChanges:
Time-based pricing shifts risk to the buyer. If the project takes longer, they pay more. That's appropriate when the uncertainty is on their side — when the scope is unclear because of their situation, not because of your process.

The incentive structure is worth examining. In time-based models, efficiency works against revenue. A faster process means a smaller invoice. Many providers unconsciously slow down to avoid the awkward conversation of "we finished faster than expected." That's a design problem, not a character flaw.
facets.whenConvenient: When scope is genuinely undefined and the client needs your judgment more than a fixed deliverable. When the buyer is sophisticated enough to evaluate your hourly value and willing to absorb scope risk. When the engagement is exploratory — diagnosis, strategy, advisory — rather than execution.
facets.examples: Consulting by the hour, advisory retainers billed on time used, day rates for embedded work, open-scope strategy engagements.
facets.traps: Using hourly pricing by default because it feels safe. It isn't. Hourly pricing caps your income at your hours, creates perverse incentives, and makes it harder to communicate value. Fixed and recurring models, when they work, are usually better for both sides. Use time-based pricing where it's genuinely the right mechanism — not as the path of least resistance.
facets.worksWellWith: Source of value — human. Touch intensity — high. Scope — undefined or exploratory. Interaction timing — synchronous.

values.name: Recurring
summary: The buyer pays repeatedly — monthly, quarterly, or annually — for continuous value. No fixed end date, no per-project invoice. Just a regular charge that continues as long as the relationship does.

Same business: "$399 per month for website management and updates." The client pays the same amount every month. You deliver ongoing value every month. The relationship continues as long as it's working.
Recurring pricing is the foundation of predictable revenue. It changes how you plan, how you hire, and how you think about the business.

facets.whatChanges:
Recurring models shift the fundamental dynamic of the business. Instead of hunting for new clients to replace finished projects, you focus on keeping existing clients enrolled. Retention becomes the growth strategy. Every month a client stays is a month you didn't have to replace them.

The challenge is justifying the renewal. In a project model, the value is delivered and the client evaluates it once. In a recurring model, the client evaluates it every time the invoice arrives. You have to keep being obviously worth it — month after month, quarter after quarter.
facets.whenConvenient: When the value is genuinely continuous — maintenance, growth, optimization, management — and not a one-time fix. When predictable revenue is a strategic priority. When the client relationship deepens with time in ways that create compounding value for both sides.
facets.examples: Website maintenance subscriptions, SEO retainers, growth partnerships, monthly advisory relationships, managed service contracts.
facets.traps: Building recurring offers around work that should be one-time. If the website is built, it's built. Charging monthly for something that's effectively done produces hollow invoices that clients eventually notice. Recurring pricing has to be backed by recurring value — something genuinely new or improved every period.
facets.worksWellWith: Effort duration — ongoing. System impact — medium to high. Commitment reversibility — high to medium. Scope — defined monthly deliverables or clear value metrics.

whyItMatters: The pricing model you choose affects the business as much as the offer itself. Fixed and variable models trade in projects. Recurring models trade in relationships. Time-based models trade in availability. Each creates a different revenue profile, a different incentive structure, and a different kind of client relationship.

Most mature businesses use more than one. A fixed entry-point offer that converts into a recurring relationship. A time-based discovery that leads to a fixed project. Understanding the models gives you the vocabulary to design that progression intentionally — not just stumble into it.

levers.name: Price framing
levers.question: What economic lens does the buyer use to evaluate the price?
intro: The price framing lever answers one question: what economic lens does the buyer use to evaluate the price?

Not how much you charge — that's the pricing model. This lever is about the buyer's mental model when they look at your number. Do they see money leaving their pocket, or money being deployed to make more money? The same price can feel like a burden or a bargain depending entirely on how it's framed.
This isn't copywriting. It's a structural choice that affects how you justify the offer, what proof you need to back it up, and who is likely to buy it.
Three core values: expense-framed, ROI-framed, and hybrid.

values.name: Expense-framed
summary: The buyer sees the payment as a cost for receiving something useful. They need a thing, you provide it, they pay for it. The evaluation is simple: is the price reasonable for what I'm getting?

For a "build a website" business: "Professional website for $2,000." The buyer needs a website. They want it to look good. They understand they have to pay for it. The comparison they're making is against other providers charging for the same thing — is $2,000 fair, cheap, or expensive for a website?
Expense framing is honest and appropriate when the value is the deliverable itself — not a downstream financial outcome.

facets.whatChanges:
Expense-framed offers are easier to justify and faster to close in low-stakes, commodity, or cosmetic markets. The buyer doesn't need to calculate ROI. They just need to feel the price is reasonable.

The ceiling is real. When the buyer is thinking "cost," they're comparing you to the cheapest alternative that delivers the same thing. Price pressure is higher, differentiation has to come from quality and trust rather than economic logic, and there's no "this pays for itself" argument to make.
facets.whenConvenient: When the outcome is primarily about utility, quality, or aesthetics — not financial return. When your buyers don't make decisions based on measurable ROI. When the market is transactional and low-touch, and a clean, fair price is what moves people.
facets.examples: Consumer purchases, cosmetic services, creative work for personal use, standard professional services in commodity markets.
facets.traps: Defaulting to expense framing because it feels more honest or modest. If your offer genuinely creates financial return, not framing it that way is leaving money on the table — both in price and in persuasion. Expense framing isn't humble. It's just incomplete, if the ROI is real.
facets.worksWellWith: Pricing model — fixed. Scope depth — shallow to medium. Buyer — consumer or small business with simple needs.

values.name: ROI-framed
summary: The buyer sees the payment as an investment expected to create measurable upside. The logic isn't "pay money, receive thing" — it's "pay money, make more money."

Same business: "Website that helps generate $10,000 or more in new revenue for $2,000." Now the buyer isn't evaluating whether $2,000 is a fair price for a website. They're evaluating whether $2,000 is a good investment for $10,000 in return. That's a completely different question — and a much easier one to say yes to.
ROI framing shifts the conversation from cost to return.

facets.whatChanges:
ROI framing raises the price ceiling significantly. If the buyer believes the offer will generate $50,000 in revenue, $5,000 feels cheap. The price is no longer compared to alternatives — it's compared to the return, and almost anything looks reasonable by that comparison.

The requirement for proof goes up equally. You can't claim ROI you can't credibly support. If your website builds don't actually generate measurable leads and revenue, ROI framing is a lie — and buyers who don't see the return will feel deceived. ROI framing works when the results are real and demonstrable.
facets.whenConvenient: When the offer creates measurable financial outcomes — more leads, higher conversion rates, more revenue, lower costs. When your buyers are business owners who make investment decisions, not just purchase decisions. When you have the track record to back up the return claim with real numbers.
facets.examples: Lead generation services, conversion rate optimization, sales training, marketing systems, anything where the output is measurable business growth.
facets.traps: Using ROI framing without the evidence to support it. "This will change your business" is not ROI framing — it's a vague promise. Real ROI framing is specific: "Our clients typically see X results within Y timeframe." If you can't say something that specific and truthful, don't frame it as ROI.
facets.worksWellWith: Source of value — human or system. System impact — medium to high. Proof requirement — case studies, data, track record. Pricing model — value-based or fixed at premium.

notes.heading: The same price, different psychology
body: The framing doesn't change the number. It changes what the buyer compares it to.
The same $2,000:
Expense frame: "A website costs $2,000." The buyer compares it to other websites.
ROI frame: "Invest $2,000 to add $10,000 in sales." The buyer compares it to $10,000.
Hybrid frame: "A premium website that improves credibility and lead generation for $2,000." The buyer compares it to both quality and outcome alternatives.
Same number. Three different buying conversations. The frame you choose determines which one you're in.

whyItMatters: Price framing is one of the highest-leverage levers for increasing price without changing the offer. If the work genuinely creates financial return, framing it as an investment rather than a cost can justify a significantly higher price — and create a buyer who feels good about paying it. Get the framing wrong and you compete on price. Get it right and you compete on value.

One final note: don't force ROI framing onto offers that can't credibly deliver measurable returns. False ROI claims are easy to make and hard to walk back. If the return isn't real and provable, expense framing is more honest — and more sustainable.

levers.name: Touch intensity
levers.question: How much direct human attention does each client receive?
intro: The touch intensity lever answers one question: how much direct human attention does each client receive?

Not what you deliver, and not how often you interact — this lever is about the density of personal involvement during delivery. How close are you to the client's experience? How quickly do you respond? How often do you step in?

When you move this lever, you're changing: how much time each client requires, how premium the offer feels, how much accountability you provide, your margins, and how many clients you can serve at once.
Three core values: high touch, medium touch, and low touch.

values.name: High touch
summary: Frequent, direct, personal involvement throughout the engagement. The client has real access to you, and you're actively present in their process.

For a "build a website" business: "I meet with you weekly, review your progress, give detailed feedback, and help you make decisions at every step." You're in it with them. They don't get stuck for long because you're close enough to see it happening.
The value comes from confidence, responsiveness, and accountability. Clients don't just buy the outcome — they buy the feeling that someone is watching and will catch problems early.

facets.whatChanges:
High touch commands higher prices. The personal involvement is the product, and it's genuinely scarce — there are only so many clients you can hold at this level without degrading the experience. Delivery load is significant, and it doesn't automate easily.

What you get in return is high satisfaction and high retention. Clients who feel taken care of don't churn. And they refer others.
facets.whenConvenient: When the outcome is high-stakes or complex. When accountability is part of what drives results. When you're charging premium prices and need the delivery to justify it. When client confidence is a key part of what you're selling.
facets.examples: Executive coaching, premium consulting, high-end DFY services, VIP programs.
facets.traps: Defaulting to high touch because you're uncomfortable letting go. Every client check-in you do out of habit rather than necessity is time you're not spending on things that actually move the work forward. Audit your touch points. Some of them are for your comfort, not theirs.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DFY or DWY. Interaction timing — synchronous or hybrid. Source of value — human. Pricing model — premium or value-based.

values.name: Medium touch
summary: Some direct support, but limited and structured. Clients know when they'll hear from you and what they can expect — it's not unlimited access, but it's not radio silence either.

Same business: "We start with a strategy call, you get two review sessions, and there's email support between them." The client has touchpoints, but they're defined. You're available, but not on-demand.
The value comes from the balance: clients feel supported without becoming dependent, and you can manage more of them without burning out.

facets.whatChanges:
Medium touch is where most scalable offers land. You maintain enough personal involvement to build trust and catch problems, while keeping your time manageable. Pricing sits below high touch but above low touch — which is appropriate, because the value delivered is genuinely between the two.

The challenge is setting expectations clearly. Medium touch only works when both sides understand the boundaries. If the client expects high touch and receives medium, they feel underserved. Over-promise and you end up in high touch at medium touch prices.
facets.whenConvenient: When the process is well-defined enough that clients don't need constant guidance. When you want to serve more clients without sacrificing the relationship. When structured support is enough to keep momentum without requiring daily involvement.
facets.examples: Productized services with defined revision rounds, group coaching with office hours, advisory retainers with monthly calls, cohort courses with limited Q&A access.
facets.traps: Letting medium touch creep into high touch over time. A "quick question" here and a "just checking in" there can add up to a full second job. Define the scope at the start, stick to it, and upsell high touch to clients who genuinely need more.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DFY at scale or DWY. Interaction timing — hybrid. Participation format — 1:group or cohort. Pricing model — flat fee or retainer.

values.name: Low touch
summary: Minimal direct involvement. The client buys and moves forward largely on their own, using what you've created.

Same business: "Buy the template pack and follow the step-by-step lessons to build it yourself." You made the materials. They do the work. Your involvement after purchase is close to zero — maybe a help center, maybe occasional support tickets, but no ongoing personal presence.
The value comes from affordability, speed, and autonomy. Clients who want to move at their own pace, on their own schedule, without depending on your availability.

facets.whatChanges:
Low touch is where scale lives. Once the content or system is built, adding another client costs you almost nothing. Margins are high. Your time is no longer the limiting factor.

The trade-off: accountability disappears. Without someone checking in, many clients won't follow through. Completion rates drop. Results become harder to guarantee. That's not a problem to fix — it's a reality to design around. Low touch offers attract self-directed clients, and you need to market to that profile specifically.
facets.whenConvenient: When the offer is designed for independent learners. When the outcome is achievable through a well-structured system without live guidance. When you want to reach price points that high or medium touch can't serve profitably.
facets.examples: Template packs, recorded courses, ebooks, self-serve tools, public content libraries.
facets.traps: Applying low touch to an offer that actually requires human accountability to work. If your clients need someone to keep them on track, a self-serve product won't deliver — no matter how good the content is. The touch intensity has to match what the outcome actually requires.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DIY. Interaction timing — async. Participation format — 1:n. Source of value — system or asset. Pricing model — one-time or subscription.

notes.heading: How to identify it
body: Ask: how much direct human attention does each client receive after buying?
Frequent and personal → High touch. Some, but structured and bounded → Medium touch. Minimal, mostly self-serve → Low touch.
Same "build a website" offer, three touch levels:
Offer A: weekly calls, detailed feedback at every step, ongoing support. → High touch.
Offer B: kickoff call, two review sessions, email support in between. → Medium touch.
Offer C: template pack plus recorded lessons, no direct involvement. → Low touch.
Same category. Very different delivery loads, price points, and client experiences.

whyItMatters: Touch intensity is one of the most direct levers on your time. Change it and you change how many clients you can serve, what you need to charge to make the math work, and how much of yourself each engagement requires.

It's also one of the levers that clients feel most acutely. They may not understand your delivery model or your pricing structure — but they know immediately whether they feel taken care of or left alone. Get the touch intensity right for your audience and your offer earns its price. Get it wrong and no amount of good content fixes the experience.

levers.name: Scope breadth
levers.question: How much of the client's journey are you responsible for?
intro: The scope breadth lever answers one question: how much of the client's journey are you responsible for?

Not how deeply you solve any one part — that's scope depth. This lever is about territory. Do you own one specific result, an entire business function, or the full chain from first contact to final outcome?
When you move this lever, you're changing: how you position the offer, how high the price ceiling goes, what the client sees as your remit, and how naturally the relationship extends over time.
Three core values: outcome, domain, and end-to-end.

values.name: Outcome
summary: You solve one specific result or deliverable. Clear scope, clear finish line.
For a "build a website" business: "I build you a landing page that converts." Not the full site. Not the brand. Not the traffic strategy. One result, done well.

Narrow doesn't mean small. A high-converting landing page for the right business is worth serious money. But outcome-level breadth means the offer doesn't try to own adjacent territory — it's precise about what it handles and what it doesn't.

facets.whatChanges:
Outcome offers are the easiest to sell. The client knows exactly what they're buying, and the value is easy to understand and evaluate. Sales cycles are short. Commitment is bounded. Trust requirements are lower because the scope is small enough to grasp in one sentence.

They're also the easiest to deliver at high quality. Focused scope means you can invest your full attention in one thing, build a repeatable process around it, and get very good at it fast.
facets.whenConvenient: When you're entering a new market and need quick wins. When you want a low-friction entry point into a client relationship. When positioning clarity matters more than revenue per client.
facets.examples: Landing page builds, homepage redesigns, website audits, speed fixes, specific feature setups.
facets.traps: Staying outcome-level when your clients consistently need more. If every engagement ends with the client asking "can you also help with X?", that's the market telling you your scope is too tight. Outcome is a great starting point — it doesn't have to be a permanent ceiling.
facets.worksWellWith: Scope depth — any. Pricing model — flat fee or one-time. Effort duration — one-time or short time-bound. Entry point in a value ladder.

values.name: Domain
summary: You handle an entire business function or area. The client isn't hiring you to solve one problem — they're handing you ownership of a whole territory.

Same business: "I take care of your whole website presence." Design, copy, strategy, ongoing management — everything related to the website as a business asset. The client doesn't have to think about the website domain because you own it.
Domain breadth is where most full-service offers live. The client gets coherent ownership of a function, not a collection of separate deliverables they have to integrate themselves.

facets.whatChanges:
Domain offers justify meaningfully higher prices than outcome offers because the value isn't one result — it's ongoing ownership and accountability across an entire function. The client isn't comparing you to a landing page builder. They're comparing you to having no one managing this area at all, or to hiring someone in-house.

Delivery complexity increases. More territory means more decisions, more coordination, and more places things can go wrong. The offer has to be well-scoped and clearly communicated — what's inside the domain, and what requires a separate conversation.
facets.whenConvenient: When your clients' problems cluster around a coherent business function. When you have enough experience to own the whole domain without degrading quality across it. When you want to increase revenue per client without expanding into territory that doesn't belong in the core offer.
facets.examples: Full website management, complete brand identity, end-to-end content production, ongoing marketing function ownership.
facets.traps: Scope creep disguised as domain breadth. "Full website presence" means different things to different clients. Define what the domain includes and what it doesn't — explicitly and upfront. Ambiguity at this level is where margin disappears.
facets.worksWellWith: Pricing model — retainer or flat fee. Effort duration — ongoing or time-bound. System impact — medium. Source of value — human or system.

values.name: End-to-end
summary: You own the complete chain connected to a business result — not just one function, but everything from the starting point to the final outcome.

Same business: "We build and run the full system that turns strangers into customers." The website is in there, but so is the traffic strategy, the funnel, the CRM, the lead follow-up, the booking system. The client isn't buying web presence. They're buying a growth engine.
At this level, you're not solving a function. You're solving a business outcome — and owning all the pieces that make it happen.

facets.whatChanges:
End-to-end offers unlock the highest price ceilings. When you're responsible for the full chain, the value conversation changes entirely. You're not competing on "what does a website cost" — you're competing on "what is a functioning client acquisition system worth to your business?" Those are different conversations with different numbers.

End-to-end also locks in the relationship. A client whose entire growth infrastructure runs through you doesn't switch easily. Retention is structural, not just relational.

The challenge is delivery. End-to-end scope requires more team, more coordination, and consistent quality across more domains. Selling it before you can reliably deliver it is the fastest way to damage a reputation.
facets.whenConvenient: When you have the infrastructure to deliver across multiple functions without degrading quality in any of them. When your clients are sophisticated enough to value the full chain. When you want to build long-term relationships around business outcomes rather than deliverables.
facets.examples: Full-service agencies, fractional CMO or COO engagements, client acquisition system builds, managed growth partnerships.
facets.traps: Over-promising on breadth and under-delivering on depth. An end-to-end offer where every component is mediocre is worse than a focused domain offer done well. Don't sell the chain until you can deliver each link at quality.
facets.worksWellWith: Pricing model — retainer or value-based. System impact — high. Touch intensity — medium to high. Scope depth — medium to deep.

notes.heading: The value ladder
body: One of the most practical uses of scope breadth is building a coherent offer stack in the same market. Clients don't have to start at the top. They can begin narrow, experience your quality, and expand as trust grows.
Same domain, ascending breadth:

- $300 landing page audit → Outcome
- $2,500 full website build → Domain
- $8,000 client acquisition system → End-to-end

Each offer is complete on its own. But they form a natural path — a client who gets the audit and loves it is already a candidate for the full build. A client who gets the build is thinking about what happens next. Scope breadth isn't just a positioning decision. It's architecture for how clients move through your business over time.

whyItMatters: Breadth determines how much territory your offer claims — and therefore how many problems it solves, how high you can price it, and how long the client relationship can last. Narrow offers are easy to sell and easy to deliver. End-to-end offers unlock higher value and deeper retention. Most businesses benefit from having both, stacked intentionally.

levers.name: Scope depth
levers.question: How far into the problem do you go?
intro: The scope depth lever answers one question: how far into the problem do you go?

Scope breadth is about how many areas you cover. Scope depth is about how thoroughly you solve one of them. You can go wide and shallow, narrow and deep, or anywhere in between. This lever controls the vertical dimension of your offer — how far down the rabbit hole you're willing and able to go.

When you move this lever, you're changing: how much expertise the delivery requires, how long it takes, how much the client's situation actually changes, how high you can price the offer, and how hard you are to compete with.
Three core values: shallow, medium, and deep.

values.name: Shallow
summary: You address the surface-level need. Fast, affordable, contained. The client gets something real and useful without a long engagement or a heavy commitment.

For a "build a website" business: "I build you a clean 3-page site so you can launch fast." Or: "I review your website, identify the main issues, and give you a fix roadmap." The problem isn't fully solved, but the client has something they can act on today.

Shallow doesn't mean low-quality. A sharp, well-executed audit that gives a client a clear action plan is a legitimate high-value offer. The depth is intentionally limited — not because you can't go further, but because the client doesn't need you to right now.

facets.whatChanges:
Shallow offers are fast to deliver and easy to scope. Sales cycles are short. Risk is low for both sides. They work especially well as entry points — a client who gets real value from a shallow engagement has every reason to come back for more.

The ceiling is real, though. There's only so much transformation you can create when you're working at the surface. If the client's core problem requires going deeper, a shallow offer leaves that problem unsolved.
facets.whenConvenient: When speed and affordability matter more than comprehensive transformation. When you want a low-friction way for new clients to experience your quality. When the client's need is genuinely surface-level and doesn't require more.
facets.examples: Website audits, homepage refreshes, template setups, starter packages, diagnostic reports, quick-launch builds.
facets.traps: Underpricing shallow work because it feels "small." A well-executed audit that saves a client from a costly redesign is worth serious money. Price based on the value of the clarity you deliver, not just the hours you spend.
facets.worksWellWith: Scope breadth — narrow. Sales cycle — short. Entry point in a value ladder. Pricing model — flat fee.

values.name: Medium
summary: You solve the problem more completely. You're not just treating the surface symptom — you're delivering a functional outcome the client can build on.

Same business: "I build a complete website optimized to generate leads." Custom pages, conversion-focused copy, mobile optimization, analytics setup, forms that actually work. The client doesn't just have a website — they have a site that's doing a job.

facets.whatChanges:
Medium depth is where most full-service offers live. The client gets a meaningful outcome, not just a starting point. You're solving the stated problem end-to-end, even if you're not going into the deeper layers underneath it.

Delivery takes longer and requires more coordination. The quality bar is higher because the client is measuring results, not just receiving a deliverable. And because the stakes are higher, the sales conversation takes longer too — the client needs more confidence before committing.
facets.whenConvenient: When the client has a real, functional need that requires a complete solution. When you have enough experience to deliver consistently at this level. When the offer's value is in solving the whole problem, not just a piece of it.
facets.examples: Custom website builds, full rebrands, complete onboarding systems, end-to-end campaign setups, comprehensive training programs.
facets.traps: Treating medium as the permanent ceiling when your clients' results suggest you could go deeper. If clients consistently come back asking for more after a medium-depth engagement, that's a signal that the underlying problem is bigger than what you're solving.
facets.worksWellWith: Scope breadth — narrow to medium. Delivery model — DFY or DWY. Source of value — human or system.

values.name: Deep
summary: You go into root causes, structural layers, and underlying systems. You're not building pages — you're solving a business performance problem.

Same business: "We rebuild your website as a revenue engine based on customer psychology and data." Or: "We redesign your entire online presence, offer positioning, funnel flow, and lead capture system so growth becomes structurally easier." The website is still there, but it's now downstream of a deeper diagnosis.

Deep work asks different questions. Not "what pages do you need?" but "why isn't this converting?" Not "what should the site look like?" but "what does your ideal client actually need to believe before they buy, and is any of that true in the current site?"

facets.whatChanges:
Deep offers require real expertise. You can't fake root-cause work — clients notice quickly when someone is going deep in form but shallow in substance. The delivery takes longer, involves more discovery, and often surfaces problems the client didn't know they had.

The upside: deep offers are the hardest to compete with. Anyone can build a website. Few people can redesign the business model that sits behind it. The deeper your scope, the more unique your offer becomes, and the harder it is for a client to find an equivalent elsewhere.
