facets.whenConvenient: When the client's problem is structural and surface solutions have already been tried. When you have the expertise and track record to operate at this level. When the client is sophisticated enough to understand and value root-cause work — and willing to pay for it.
facets.examples: Strategic repositioning engagements, conversion rate optimization programs, full funnel architecture, organizational design, long-term advisory relationships.
facets.traps: Going deep without the authority to back it up. Clients sense when someone is improvising at this level. Deep work requires not just more effort but a different kind of knowledge — systems thinking, pattern recognition across many clients, and the ability to connect surface problems to underlying causes. Build to that level before you sell it.
facets.worksWellWith: Source of value — human. Touch intensity — high. Scope breadth — narrow to medium (deep and broad simultaneously is rare and hard to deliver). Pricing model — value-based.

notes.heading: Depth and breadth together
body: The two scope levers combine to define the overall shape of your offer. Four useful combinations:

- Narrow + shallow: fast, affordable, entry-level. A landing page audit. Easy to sell, easy to deliver.
- Narrow + deep: highly specialized. A landing page rebuilt from scratch with full conversion research and testing. Premium positioning in a specific domain.
- Broad + shallow: wide coverage, light execution. A full digital setup with basic implementation across many channels. Often productized.
- Broad + deep: rare and demanding. A full business growth system built and optimized thoroughly across every channel. Requires significant team, time, and client trust.
Most offers live in the middle of this grid. The value of naming your position is that it becomes a deliberate choice rather than an accident.

whyItMatters: Depth is one of the most direct levers on pricing power and competitive resistance. The deeper you go, the fewer people can do what you do, the more transformation you create, and the harder your offer is to compare against alternatives. Moving down the depth axis is often the most reliable path to charging more in the same domain — without necessarily covering more ground.

levers.name: System impact
levers.question: How much does the client's business actually change because of this offer?
intro: The system impact lever answers one question: how much does the client's business actually change because of this offer?

Not how deep the work goes — that's scope depth. Not how many areas it covers — that's scope breadth. This lever measures something different: the ripple effect. Once the offer is delivered, how much of the client's underlying system — their habits, processes, team behavior, revenue engine — is different from what it was before?

When you move this lever, you're changing: how the client perceives your ROI, how sticky the relationship is, how easy you are to replace, and what kind of referrals and retention the offer generates.
Three core values: low, medium, and high impact.

values.name: Low impact
summary: The offer creates a standalone result. The deliverable is real and useful, but the business operates mostly the same way after receiving it.

For a "build a website" business: "I build your website and hand it over." The client has a new website. That's a genuine improvement. But their sales process, their lead capture, their team workflows — none of that changed. The website sits alongside the existing system without integrating into it.
Low-impact offers aren't a problem. They're the right design for many situations. A client who just needs a clean site to point people to doesn't need a system overhaul.

facets.whatChanges: Low-impact offers are easier to scope, faster to deliver, and less likely to surface unexpected complexity. The client knows what they're getting and can evaluate it easily. The trade-off: perceived ROI is limited to the deliverable itself, and switching to a different provider next time carries little cost.
facets.whenConvenient: When the client's real need is a standalone output. When they're not ready or willing to change their underlying operations. When you want a fast, contained engagement that demonstrates your quality without requiring deep integration.
facets.examples: Website builds handed off at completion, logo design, template installation, one-time audits, standalone content assets.
facets.traps: Delivering low-impact work when the client's actual problem requires a system change. If the root issue is that no one is following up with leads, a beautiful website doesn't fix that. Diagnosing system impact early helps you set accurate expectations — and sometimes reveals that the client needs more than they're asking for.
facets.worksWellWith: Scope breadth — narrow. Scope depth — shallow to medium. Delivery model — DFY. Engagement duration — short.

values.name: Medium impact
summary: The offer improves connected workflows and creates ongoing operational benefits. The business doesn't just have a new asset — it works better because of how that asset connects to everything else.

Same business: "We build your website and connect it to lead capture, bookings, and reporting." Now when someone visits the site, there's a form that goes into a CRM. Appointments book automatically. The client can see what's working. The website isn't just a brochure — it's doing work in the business every day.

facets.whatChanges:
Medium-impact offers generate ongoing value beyond the delivery date. The client feels the benefit every time the system runs — every lead that comes in automatically, every booking that self-schedules, every report that doesn't require manual assembly. That recurring value justifies higher prices and creates a natural reason to maintain the relationship.

It also raises the switching cost. Ripping out a website is easy. Ripping out a website, CRM integration, booking flow, and analytics setup is a project. The deeper the integration, the stickier the engagement.
facets.whenConvenient: When the client's problem is operational as much as it is a deliverable problem. When you have the expertise to connect the pieces well, not just build them individually. When recurring value — not just point-in-time value — is part of what justifies the price.
facets.examples: Website plus CRM integration, content management systems with workflow automation, onboarding flows connected to email sequences, analytics dashboards built into daily operations.
facets.traps: Building integrations the client won't actually use. A CRM connection that no one checks is medium effort for low impact. Before connecting systems, make sure the client has the habits and team capacity to benefit from the connection. Unused infrastructure isn't an upgrade — it's complexity without payoff.
facets.worksWellWith: Scope breadth — medium. Scope depth — medium. Touch intensity — medium. Delivery model — DFY or DWY.

values.name: High impact
summary: The offer changes core business mechanics. The client's revenue engine, decision-making process, or operating infrastructure is fundamentally different after this engagement than it was before.

Same business: "We redesign your website, funnel, messaging, and operating flow so client acquisition becomes systematic." The website is one piece. But the offer also repositioned how the business talks about itself, rebuilt the sales pipeline, automated follow-up, and gave the team a new process for handling inbound leads. Now the business grows differently — not just faster, but through a different mechanism.
High-impact offers don't just solve a problem. They change how the business solves problems in that domain going forward.

facets.whatChanges:
High-impact work generates the strongest ROI perception, the deepest retention, and the most valuable referrals. Clients who experience real systemic change don't forget who caused it. They refer others, they come back, and they trust your judgment on the next problem.

It also requires the most from both sides. You need the expertise to understand the system, not just the deliverable. The client needs to be willing to change — not just receive — which not every client is ready for. High-impact engagements require buy-in, time, and organizational capacity that some clients simply don't have yet.
facets.whenConvenient: When the client's problem is structural. When they've already tried surface-level solutions and they didn't hold. When the client is sophisticated enough to understand what systemic change means and willing to commit to the process of getting there.
facets.examples: Full business repositioning, sales infrastructure rebuilds, client acquisition system design, operational redesigns, long-term strategic advisory.
facets.traps: Promising high impact but delivering medium. High-impact work is measurable — the client's business either operates differently or it doesn't. If you're selling system transformation but the client still has the same habits and workflows six months later, the impact didn't land regardless of what was delivered. Only sell the impact level you can actually produce.
facets.worksWellWith: Scope depth — deep. Touch intensity — high. Source of value — human. Pricing model — value-based. Engagement duration — long.

notes.heading: Scope depth vs. system impact
body: These two levers measure different things and don't move together automatically.
Scope depth is about how thoroughly you solve a specific problem. System impact is about how much of the surrounding system changes as a result.
A deep copy rewrite on a single homepage page: deep scope, potentially low system impact if the rest of the funnel stays the same.
A basic CRM integration that automates follow-up: medium scope, potentially high system impact if it changes how the sales team operates every day.

You can go deep on a narrow piece of the problem and create high system impact if that piece is load-bearing. You can also go broad and deep and create surprisingly low system impact if the client doesn't change how they operate around what you built.
The question to ask before designing any offer: if this works exactly as planned, what else changes automatically? That answer tells you the system impact level.

whyItMatters: Clients pay for deliverables. They remember — and talk about — transformation. The higher the system impact, the harder you are to replace, the stronger the ROI story, and the more naturally the relationship extends over time. Moving this lever up is often the highest-leverage change you can make to an offer's perceived and actual value.

levers.name: Buyer participation intensity
levers.question: How much does the client have to do for this to work?
intro: The buyer participation intensity lever answers one question: how much does the client have to do for this to work?

Not how much you do — that's touch intensity. This lever is specifically about the client's workload. How much effort, time, input, and ongoing involvement does the buyer need to contribute to get the result they paid for?

When you move this lever, you're changing: how convenient the offer feels, what kind of buyer it attracts, how often clients follow through, how much support they need, and how consistent the outcomes are across different clients.
Three core values: low, medium, and high participation.

values.name: Low participation
summary: The buyer has minimal involvement. They hand things off and receive the result.

For a "build a website" business: "Send us your details and we handle the rest." The client fills an intake form, sends the logo and some photos, approves the draft, and signs off at the end. They're present at a few key moments but not doing any real work.

The value here is convenience. Busy buyers — the ones who have the money but not the time — gravitate toward low-participation offers. They're not paying for the experience of building. They're paying to have it done.

facets.whatChanges:
Low-participation offers convert well to buyers who have the money and the desire but not the bandwidth. The sales conversation is short: "You do nothing, we handle it." That's a simple, attractive promise.

The challenge is that results become entirely your responsibility. If the website doesn't perform, the client didn't fail to show up — you did. That's appropriate accountability when you're charging a premium, but it means your delivery has to hold up without the client's input making it better.
facets.whenConvenient: When the buyer's time is genuinely scarce and that scarcity is a real constraint, not an excuse. When your process is tight enough to produce good results with minimal client involvement. When you want to attract high-value buyers who pay for outcomes, not experiences.
facets.examples: Full-service agencies, productized DFY services, white-glove packages, concierge programs.
facets.traps: Designing for low participation when the outcome actually requires client input to be good. A website that perfectly reflects the client's brand, voice, and positioning requires real information about those things. If you skip the discovery because it's "low participation," you often end up building something technically complete but strategically wrong — and revisions undo the efficiency you were trying to create.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DFY. Touch intensity — high. Source of value — human or system. Scope breadth — narrow to medium.

values.name: Medium participation
summary: The buyer contributes meaningful input at key moments but isn't doing sustained work throughout the engagement.

Same business: "We guide the project, but you'll review pages and provide feedback weekly." The client shows up for a kickoff call, reviews drafts, gives feedback at defined checkpoints, and makes decisions when decisions are needed. Between those touchpoints, the work moves forward without them.

This is the most common configuration in well-run service businesses. The client has real ownership without being in the weeds. You have the information you need to do the work well without scheduling around a passive buyer.

facets.whatChanges:
Medium participation balances quality and convenience. The client's input makes the output better — decisions get made faster, feedback loops stay short, and the client feels genuinely involved in something that's being built for them specifically.

The friction point is coordination. Medium participation requires the client to actually show up at the agreed moments. When they don't — when feedback is late, when the kickoff gets rescheduled, when approvals drag — the whole engagement slows down. Clear expectations upfront about what "medium participation" means in practice are essential.
facets.whenConvenient: When client input genuinely improves the output. When the buyer has time for checkpoints but not for sustained daily involvement. When you want a collaborative relationship without making the buyer responsible for doing the work.
facets.examples: Project-based services with defined review rounds, coaching programs with between-session assignments, advisory retainers with monthly input sessions.
facets.traps: Letting medium drift into low when the client goes quiet. If the client stops showing up for reviews and you keep building anyway, you're doing low-participation work without the pricing or clarity to match it. Hold the structure — delay before proceeding without the input you need.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DFY or DWY. Touch intensity — medium. Interaction timing — hybrid. Participation format — 1:1 or small group.

values.name: High participation
summary: The buyer actively works throughout the process. They're not a passenger — they're a co-builder, learner, or implementer. The result comes from their effort as much as yours.

Same business: "We coach you to build the website with us, and you complete tasks each week." The client writes copy, gathers assets, makes design decisions, and implements pieces themselves — with your guidance at every step. At the end, they have a website they understand and can maintain, built largely by their own hands.
High participation is where the strongest learning outcomes live. When the client does the work, they build the skill. They don't just have a website — they know how to have a website.

facets.whatChanges:
High-participation offers attract a specific kind of buyer: someone who wants the skill, not just the result. Someone motivated, self-directed, and willing to invest their time alongside their money. These buyers tend to be deeply engaged and get strong outcomes when they follow through.

The conversion challenge is real. Many buyers think they want high participation until they realize what it actually requires. The gap between "I want to learn this" and "I'm willing to do homework every week for six weeks" is wide. Your marketing has to attract buyers who genuinely match the participation level, not just aspire to it.
facets.whenConvenient: When learning is part of the value. When the buyer's involvement in the process improves the outcome in ways you can't replicate by doing it for them. When you want to serve self-directed, motivated clients who are less likely to churn or blame you for lack of results.
facets.examples: Cohort courses with weekly deliverables, coaching programs with implementation requirements, workshops with between-session work, guided DIY builds.
facets.traps: Underestimating how many buyers will drop off. High-participation offers have inherently lower completion rates. Not because the offer is bad — because life gets in the way, and motivation fades. Design for this: build in accountability mechanisms, celebrate early wins, reduce friction on the hardest tasks. And make peace with the fact that not everyone finishes. That's not a design failure. It's the nature of high-participation work.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DWY or DIY. Touch intensity — medium to high. Interaction timing — hybrid. Source of value — human or system.

notes.heading: Touch intensity vs. buyer participation intensity
body: These two levers are easy to conflate. They're not the same.
Touch intensity measures how much attention you give the client. Buyer participation intensity measures how much work the client puts in. Both can be high or low independently.
A concierge DFY service: high touch (you're constantly involved), low buyer participation (the client barely has to show up).
A self-paced course with no support: low touch, high buyer participation. The client does all the work. You're nowhere to be found.
A guided cohort build: medium to high touch, high buyer participation. You're present and engaged, and so are they.

The combination matters. High touch plus high buyer participation creates the most intense engagement — and often the best outcomes, if the client is ready for it. Low touch plus low participation is the lightest possible offer. Most offers live somewhere in between, and the right combination depends on who you're serving and what outcome you're promising.

whyItMatters: Many buyers say they want transformation but actually buy convenience. Understanding participation intensity helps you match the offer to what the client will actually do — not just what they say they want. The gap between those two things is where unmet expectations and refund requests come from. Get this lever right and client satisfaction goes up, outcomes improve, and your reputation compounds.

levers.name: Effort duration
levers.question: How long does the commitment last?
intro: The effort duration lever answers one question: how long does the commitment last?

Not how intense the work is during that time — that's touch intensity. Not how many areas it covers — that's scope breadth. This lever is specifically about the time structure of the engagement. When does it start, when does it end, and is there a fixed end at all?

When you move this lever, you're changing: how predictable your revenue is, what kind of buyer commitment you require, how you structure delivery, and whether the client relationship is transactional or ongoing.
Three core values: one-time, time-bound, and ongoing.

values.name: One-time
summary: A single delivery event or short burst of effort, then it ends. One clear scope, one clear finish line.
For a "build a website" business: "We build your website this week and hand it over." The client buys once. You deliver once. The engagement closes when the deliverable is complete.
One-time offers are the cleanest to sell and the simplest to deliver. The buyer knows exactly what they're committing to, and so do you.

facets.whatChanges:
One-time offers create urgency in the sale — the client is buying a thing, not entering a relationship. That makes the conversion easier in some ways (low commitment) and harder in others (no recurring revenue, no natural next step unless you create one).

They also create the revenue volatility problem that plagues most freelancers and agencies. When every project ends, every project has to be replaced. One-time offers require constant new client acquisition to sustain income.
facets.whenConvenient: When the value is inherently contained — an audit, a sprint, a specific deliverable. When you want low-friction entry points that let new clients experience your quality without a long commitment. When you're building a value ladder and need a bottom rung.
facets.examples: Website audits, landing page builds, one-day design sprints, homepage redesigns, one-time consulting sessions.
facets.traps: Defaulting to one-time without thinking about what comes next. A one-time offer with no natural next step leaves money and relationships on the table. Even if the engagement is genuinely one-time, you should know what a client who loved it would logically buy afterward.
facets.worksWellWith: Scope breadth — narrow. Scope depth — shallow to medium. Pricing model — flat fee. Entry point in a value ladder.

values.name: Time-bound
summary: The offer runs for a defined period — a set start date, a set end date, milestones in between. The client commits to a specific window of time, not just a single event.

Same business: "Over 6 weeks, we build and launch your website together." There's a kickoff, there are weekly check-ins, there are milestones along the way, and there's a launch date everyone is working toward. The structure creates momentum and accountability that a one-time sprint can't always sustain.

facets.whatChanges:
Time-bound offers create a different psychological contract with the buyer. They're committing to a journey, not just a purchase. That raises the stakes — for both sides — and tends to produce stronger outcomes because the structure forces progress.

From a business perspective, time-bound engagements give you a planning horizon. You know what you're delivering and when. Revenue for the period is predictable once the client is enrolled. The challenge is filling the pipeline with new time-bound engagements before the current cohort finishes.
facets.whenConvenient: When the outcome requires sustained effort over time rather than a single burst. When milestones and accountability are part of what makes the offer work. When you want to build a deeper relationship without committing to an indefinite engagement.
facets.examples: Coaching programs, build-with-you projects, cohort courses, fixed-term advisory engagements, sprint-based consulting.
facets.traps: Over-engineering the timeline. Six weeks is a commitment — for you and for the client. If the work could realistically be done in two weeks but you've stretched it to six for structure's sake, the engagement feels slow and the client loses momentum halfway through. Match the duration to what the work actually requires.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DWY or DFY. Participation format — cohort or 1:1. Interaction timing — hybrid. Buyer participation intensity — medium to high.

values.name: Ongoing
summary: The offer continues indefinitely, renewing until canceled or explicitly concluded. There's no fixed end date built into the original commitment.
Same business: "We manage and improve your website every month." The client pays monthly. You deliver monthly. The relationship continues as long as it's generating value for both sides.

Ongoing offers are the foundation of predictable revenue. You know, at the start of each month, approximately what you'll earn. That changes how you plan, how you hire, and how you invest in the business.

facets.whatChanges:
Ongoing relationships compound in ways one-time and time-bound engagements can't. You get better at serving the client over time because you understand their context more deeply. They get more value over time for the same price because your quality improves without the cost going up. Trust deepens. Referrals come more naturally.

The challenge is churn. An ongoing offer that stops generating obvious value gets canceled. You have to keep earning the renewal — not just at the start, but every month. That requires consistent delivery, visible results, and a relationship the client would actually miss if it ended.
facets.whenConvenient: When the value is genuinely continuous — maintenance, optimization, growth, management. When recurring revenue is a strategic priority. When the client relationship benefits from long-term context that a short engagement can't build.
facets.examples: Website maintenance retainers, SEO and content programs, growth partnerships, monthly advisory relationships, managed service subscriptions.
facets.traps: Selling ongoing work that the client should really only need once. If the problem is fixed after month three and you're billing month four for diminishing marginal value, the client will notice before you do. Structure ongoing offers around problems that are genuinely continuous — growth, optimization, maintenance — not around problems that have natural completion points.
facets.worksWellWith: Pricing model — subscription or retainer. System impact — medium to high. Scope breadth — medium to broad. Engagement structure — defined monthly deliverables or clear value metrics.

notes.heading: How long the game lasts
body: The same domain can support all three effort durations simultaneously, stacked as a value ladder:
One-time: homepage redesign. The client gets something fast and contained.
Time-bound: 6-week full website project. The client gets a complete outcome with structure and accountability.
Ongoing: monthly growth and maintenance. The client gets continuous improvement and a partner who knows their business.
Each rung attracts a different buyer at a different stage of readiness. Some clients start at the bottom and climb. Others enter at the level that fits their situation right now.

whyItMatters: Effort duration shapes your business model as much as it shapes the client experience. One-time offers produce lumpy revenue and require constant new business. Ongoing offers produce predictable income and compound relationships. Time-bound offers sit in between — structured, finite, and repeatable. The right mix depends on where your business is and where you want it to go.

levers.name: Commitment reversibility
levers.question: How easy is it for the buyer to exit after saying yes?
intro: The commitment reversibility lever answers one question: how easy is it for the buyer to exit after saying yes?

Not how long the engagement lasts — that's effort duration. This lever is about perceived risk and flexibility. Once the client commits, how trapped do they feel? How difficult is it to change course, pause, or walk away if things don't go as expected?

When you move this lever, you're changing: how easy the first yes is, how much trust the buyer needs before buying, what happens to your revenue when clients leave, and how strong your lock-in is once they're in.
Three core values: high reversibility, medium reversibility, and low reversibility.

values.name: High reversibility
summary: Easy to stop, cancel, switch, or back out. The buyer can change their mind without much consequence.

For a "build a website" business: "Monthly website support, cancel anytime." The client pays month to month. If they're unhappy, they stop paying. No penalties, no long notice period, no sunk cost holding them in place.
High reversibility lowers the barrier to the first yes. The buyer doesn't have to be certain before committing — they just have to be willing to try. That's a much easier threshold to clear.

facets.whatChanges:
High-reversibility offers convert more easily. The risk of being wrong feels manageable, so buyers say yes faster and with less due diligence. That's especially valuable when you're building a new audience or trying to get first clients.

The trade-off is churn. When leaving is easy, leaving happens more often. Clients who are slightly dissatisfied or simply distracted cancel instead of pushing through. Your revenue depends on continuously delivering visible value — the moment you stop being obviously worth it, the relationship ends.
facets.whenConvenient: When you're entering a new market and need to lower the barrier to trial. When your service generates ongoing, demonstrable value that keeps clients enrolled without contractual pressure. When trust is the primary barrier to purchase and you want to remove the risk objection entirely.
facets.examples: Month-to-month subscriptions, pay-per-session models, cancel-anytime memberships, small trial projects, satisfaction guarantees.
facets.traps: Using high reversibility as a substitute for quality. "Cancel anytime" doesn't fix a weak offer — it just makes it easier to cancel faster. The clients who stay in a high-reversibility model do so because they genuinely want to. Earn that, don't assume it.
facets.worksWellWith: Effort duration — ongoing. Pricing model — subscription or retainer. Entry-level offers in a value ladder.

values.name: Medium reversibility
summary: Some commitment exists, but there are clear exit points or manageable switching costs. The buyer isn't locked in, but changing course requires deliberate effort.

Same business: "6-week website project with milestone approvals." The client commits to the full engagement, but at each milestone they review and approve before work continues. If something goes wrong early, there's a natural checkpoint to address it before more resources are invested.
Medium reversibility signals seriousness. Both sides are committing to something real, not just dipping a toe in.

facets.whatChanges:
Medium-reversibility offers attract more committed buyers. Someone who agrees to a defined project with checkpoints is more invested than someone paying month to month. They're more likely to show up, provide good input, and see the engagement through — which tends to produce better outcomes.

The sales conversation is slightly longer. Buyers do more due diligence before committing to something that's harder to exit. That's appropriate — you want clients who've genuinely decided, not clients who signed up impulsively and will question everything.
facets.whenConvenient: When the offer requires sustained effort from both sides to work well. When you want buyers who are committed to the process, not just sampling. When the project has natural phases that create legitimate review points without making exit feel punitive.
facets.examples: Fixed-scope project engagements, cohort programs with milestone reviews, staged-payment packages, time-bound advisory agreements.
facets.traps: Designing medium reversibility as a way to trap clients rather than structure the engagement. Milestone approvals should be genuine checkpoints where both sides assess progress — not fine-print gates that make cancellation technically possible but practically difficult. Clients who feel manipulated don't come back and don't refer.
facets.worksWellWith: Effort duration — time-bound. Delivery model — DFY or DWY. Buyer participation intensity — medium to high.

values.name: Low reversibility
summary: Hard to undo after buying. The commitment is substantial, the switching cost is high, or the structure makes exit genuinely difficult.

Same business: "12-month managed website contract with a custom proprietary stack." The client signs for a year. Switching away means migrating off a platform built specifically for this engagement, rebuilding integrations, and finding a new provider who understands the setup. Even if they want to leave, the cost of leaving is significant.

Low reversibility isn't inherently predatory. Annual contracts in exchange for lower pricing are a legitimate trade. Proprietary stacks can be genuinely better than alternatives. The question is whether the lock-in is earned by real value or manufactured by friction.

facets.whatChanges:
Low-reversibility offers are harder to sell — buyers think longer and scrutinize more before committing to something they can't easily undo. Sales cycles lengthen. Trust requirements go up. You need more proof, more reputation, or more relationship before the buyer takes the leap.

What you get in return: stable revenue and reduced churn. A client locked into an annual contract doesn't cancel impulsively. That predictability is genuinely valuable for planning and investment.
facets.whenConvenient: When the offer generates clearly compounding value over time that justifies the commitment. When switching costs are a natural function of the offer's depth, not an artificial barrier. When the client relationship is well-established enough that the commitment feels natural rather than pressured.
facets.examples: Annual contracts, large upfront custom builds, enterprise SaaS with implementation dependencies, long-term managed service agreements.
facets.traps: Building lock-in as a substitute for ongoing value. If the main reason clients stay is that leaving is hard, that's not a strong business — it's a hostage situation. Low reversibility works when clients would choose to stay even if leaving were easy. Everything else is borrowed time.
facets.worksWellWith: Effort duration — ongoing or long time-bound. System impact — medium to high. Pricing model — annual or value-based.

notes.heading: Reversibility vs. duration
body: These two levers are easy to conflate. They're independent.
Effort duration is about how long the engagement runs. Commitment reversibility is about how easy it is to leave during that run.
A 12-month contract with monthly cancellation rights: long duration, high reversibility.
A one-time $20,000 non-refundable project: short duration, low reversibility.

The combination you choose sends a clear signal to buyers. Long duration plus low reversibility says: "This is a serious commitment." Short duration plus high reversibility says: "Try it, see how it feels." Both are valid. The right combination depends on who your buyer is and what they need to feel confident enough to say yes.

whyItMatters: Reversibility is one of the most underrated levers in the framework — not because it's subtle, but because most people set it by default rather than by design. They use annual contracts because that's what they've seen others do, or cancel-anytime because they're afraid of commitment objections. Neither of those is a strategy.

Set your reversibility deliberately. Ask what level of commitment your buyer actually needs to get a good outcome, and what level of risk they need to feel comfortable enough to start. Then design the offer around both answers.

levers.name: Authority dynamic
levers.question: What role do you occupy in relation to the buyer's decisions?
intro: The authority dynamic lever answers one question: what role do you occupy in relation to the buyer's decisions?

Not what you deliver, and not how involved you are — those are other levers. This one is about power and judgment. When an important decision needs to be made in the engagement, who makes it? Does the client direct and you execute? Do you guide them toward their own conclusion? Or do they defer to your expertise?

When you move this lever, you're changing: how you're positioned in the market, what pricing power you can command, what the sales conversation sounds like, how decisions get made during delivery, and how the client measures whether the engagement was successful.
Three core values: provider, coach, and expert.

values.name: Provider
summary: You are primarily an executor. The buyer decides what they want. You deliver it.

For a "build a website" business: "Tell me what website you want and I'll build it." The client shows up with references, specific requests, a list of pages, a color palette. You take the brief and produce the output. Your job is to be capable and responsive — not to question the strategy behind the request.
Provider positioning is honest and legitimate. Many buyers know what they want and simply need competent execution. They're not hiring your judgment. They're hiring your hands.

facets.whatChanges:
Provider offers are easier to understand and faster to buy. The client doesn't need to trust your strategic thinking — they just need to trust your execution. That lowers the sales barrier, especially for buyers who've already figured out what they need.

The trade-off is commodification. When you're executing someone else's decisions, you're replaceable by anyone who can execute as well or cheaper. Price pressure is structural, not circumstantial. And scope creep becomes a constant risk — clients who own the decisions also tend to change them, often without considering the cost.
facets.whenConvenient: When the buyer genuinely knows what they want and doesn't need or value your strategic input. When execution quality is the differentiator, not judgment. When speed and responsiveness matter more than transformation.
facets.examples: Freelance development, design execution from provided specs, implementation of a given strategy, support within a defined system.
facets.traps: Defaulting to provider positioning because it feels easier or more humble. Agreeing to execute whatever the client asks — even when you can see the strategy is wrong — protects you from conflict in the short term and produces bad outcomes in the long term. Those bad outcomes get attributed to your execution, not to the brief you followed. Staying silent when you see something wrong isn't professional neutrality. It's a disservice.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DFY. Source of value — system. Touch intensity — medium to low. Scope — clearly defined upfront.

values.name: Coach
summary: The buyer makes the important decisions, but you support and shape their thinking along the way. You ask the right questions, offer frameworks, review choices, and keep them accountable. The direction is theirs. The guidance is yours.

Same business: "I'll guide you step by step so you can build the right website for your business." The client is in the driver's seat — they decide what pages they want, what the copy says, how the design should feel. But you're beside them as they figure it out, asking questions that surface better answers, flagging things that don't add up, and keeping the project moving when they get stuck.
Coach positioning is inherently empowering. The client doesn't just end up with a website — they end up with the understanding to manage and improve it. That's a different kind of value.

facets.whatChanges:
Coach positioning requires genuine client engagement. The outcome depends on the client's participation, not just your delivery. When clients are motivated and self-directed, the results are often better than what a provider relationship produces — the client's ownership of the decisions creates commitment to making them work.

When clients aren't engaged, the model breaks. A client who wants to be coached but never does the thinking, never shows up with real input, is a coaching relationship that can't function. Screening for buyer participation intensity is critical when you're operating as a coach.
facets.whenConvenient: When you're delivering a DWY or DIY offer. When the client's ownership of the outcome matters to the result — either because they need to maintain and evolve it, or because their buy-in is part of what makes it work. When you want to build client capability alongside the deliverable.
facets.examples: Business coaching, skills-based education, guided implementation programs, advisory with frameworks rather than answers.
facets.traps: Coaching buyers who secretly want to be told what to do. Not every client who signs up for a guided process is ready to own their decisions. Some want the feeling of agency without the work it requires. If you sense a client is waiting for you to just tell them what to do, have that conversation directly — either shift the dynamic to expert mode, or clarify what coach mode actually requires from them.
facets.worksWellWith: Delivery model — DWY or DIY. Buyer participation intensity — high. Interaction timing — synchronous or hybrid. Source of value — human.

values.name: Expert
summary: The client trusts your judgment. You assess the situation, define the best path, make key recommendations, and lead the decisions. The buyer's job is to implement or approve — not to originate the strategy.

Same business: "I'll determine the highest-converting website strategy and build what your business needs." The client isn't coming with a brief. They're bringing a problem — "we're not getting enough leads" or "our site doesn't reflect what we actually do" — and deferring to you to figure out what the solution should be.
Expert positioning is the highest-authority configuration. The client buys your certainty as much as your capability.

facets.whatChanges:
Expert positioning enables premium pricing. When the client is paying for your judgment — not just your execution — the value equation changes entirely. You're not competing with other providers on price. You're competing on the quality of your thinking and the strength of your track record.

It also accelerates decisions during the engagement. When the client trusts your recommendations, the back-and-forth shrinks. You say what should be done, they approve, it gets done. That efficiency is one of the hidden benefits of expert positioning that rarely shows up in the pitch but matters enormously in delivery.

The requirement is real. Expert positioning is only credible when you actually have the expertise and track record to back it up. A provider calling themselves an expert is a fragile claim — it takes one wrong recommendation to collapse it. The authority has to be earned before it's claimed.
facets.whenConvenient: When the client's problem requires diagnosis before solution. When you have a clear pattern-recognition advantage — you've seen this kind of business, this kind of problem, many times before, and you know what works. When you want to command premium prices and attract buyers who value certainty over control.
facets.examples: Strategy consulting, fractional executive roles, specialized advisory, high-end design and positioning work, any engagement where the buyer is paying for a point of view.
facets.traps: Claiming expert authority without the track record to support it. Buyers can feel the difference between genuine expertise and performed confidence. Build the authority first — through provider and coach engagements that produce results — then let it graduate into expert positioning naturally. It's earned, not declared.
facets.worksWellWith: Source of value — human. Touch intensity — high. Scope depth — deep. Pricing model — value-based or premium fixed. Price framing — ROI.

notes.heading: Who owns the important decisions
body: The simple version: ask who owns the important decisions in the engagement.
Buyer decides, you execute → Provider. You're the hands.
Buyer decides, you support the thinking → Coach. You're the guide.
You recommend, buyer approves → Expert. You're the brain.
Same offer, three authority dynamics:
"Tell me what to build and I'll build it." → Provider.
"I'll help you figure out what to build, step by step." → Coach.
"I'll assess your business and tell you what should be built." → Expert.
Same domain, same deliverable. Different relationship to judgment, different price ceiling, different client profile.

whyItMatters: Authority dynamic shapes your positioning more than almost any other lever. It determines how you're perceived before the sale, how decisions are made during delivery, and what the client remembers afterward. Providers get replaced. Coaches get appreciated. Experts get referred. Moving up the authority axis is one of the most reliable paths to higher prices, better clients, and a reputation that compounds.
